C har act er 
Description: This character Is 
the all·around man in charge. 
He's the responsible hero who, 
even If he isn 't pany leader. 
thinks like one: He's always 
thinking about the team's goals 
and how to Implement them. 
the safety of his friends, the 
strategy of the bad guys, and so 
forth, He may be happy to be the 
leader·type. or may be a reluc· 
tant leader: either way. his 
friends look to him for direction. 
Naturally. you s hould on ly 
choose this personality type for 
vour character Ifyou're to make 
inc elTort to do all that thinking 
and planning. and are willing 
for your character LO be respon· 
sible for others. 
Best Suited For: In terms of

ate: Chaotic characters tend La 
be more spontaneous than the

otic Natura] Leader, your OM 
may choose to allow you to play

are appropriate to this personal· 
Ity type, especially the Cavalier 
and Noble Warrior. In a party 
representing several different 
Warrior Kits. though. the Ber· 
serker and Savage shouldn't be 
Natural Leaders: Other charae· 
ter types don't tend to trust 
them as leaders. 
In Combat Situations: The 
Natural Leader. whether he 
wants to or not, is always think· 
ing In combat and trying to dl· 
rect his allies' activities. He 
makes sure that there's a good 
mix of ranged to melee combal. 
that shield walls don't have big 
holes In them. that when a 
friend falls there's someone to 
drag him behind the lines and 
another to take his place, If a 
player with a Natural Leader 
character doesn't do this, and 
simply jumps right into combat 
without any Intent to think 
about or direct things, the OM 
should assign him temporary 
min uses to hit and damage: 
This reflects the fact that the 
characler is troubled by his fail· 
ure to lead, even If the player 
Isn't, 
In Role-Playing Situations: 
The Natural Leader is a very re· 
sponslble character, but beyond 
that, there Is no limitation on 
the way he should be played in 
role· playing situations. He may 
be grim or humorous, straight· 
forward or deceitful. arrogant or 
modest, as the player decides, 
He wiU onen be the negotiator 
for the party, but this ISI1', re, 
quired of him . 
The Sneaky Thinker 
C h a r ac ter 
Description: This character 
lives and loves to outwit other 
characters. He loves tricking 
money out of people, outwitting 
opponents in combat. persuad· 
ing people to help him against 
their beller sensibilities, and so 
forth . It's best for a campaign 
when the Sneaky Thinker Is a 
good friend of the rest of the pes 
and so mostly turns his talents 
on NPCs; the D~ I needs to make 
sure that the campaign docsn', 
turn into an endless succession 
of Incidents of the Sneaky 
Thinker tricking members of 
his own party. 
Best Suited For: In terms of 
alignment. Neutral and Chaotic 
characters are best suited to be 
Sneaky Thinkers, as Lawful 
characters of len have more re· 
spect for the rights of others: 
but. again. with a good enough 
rationale pro"lded, t.he OM may 
allow himself to be convinced 
that a Lawful character should 
be a Sneaky Thinker. Most War· 
rlor Kits are equally suited to 
the Sneaky Thinker character: 
the Berserker. however. Is an In· 
appropriate choice and should 
not be taken. 
43 
In Combat Situations: The 
Sneaky Thinker hates to engage 
enemies In melee. He doesn't 
mind being a ranged fighter, but 
he'd really prefer to be doing 
something clever or spectacular: Sneaking around the fight to 
come up on the enemy's un· 
guarded rea r. loosening rocks 
on the hillside above to drop 
down on the enemy, persuading 
one of the enemy's allies to tum 
on the enemy, anything that will 
demonstrate his mental superIority over the enemy (and 
maybe over his allies as well), 
It's up to the player whether the 
character Is actually cowardly 
or not: Sneaky Thinkers can be 
as brave as the bravest warrior 
in a pinch, If that's the playcr's 
conception of the character. 
In Role-Playing Situations: 
The Sneaky Thinker is often the 
party's negotiator. as he loves 
talking with other characters 
and gelling the best possible 
deal for himself and his friends. 
In most role·playlng situations, 
the Sneaky 
Thinker 
will. 
chameleon·llke. adapt himself 
to the personality of the person 
he's talking to. concealing hls 
true thoughts and emoUons be· 
hind the mask he thinks Is most 
appealing to Lhe other charac· 
ter, II's very hard to find out 
what the Sneaky Thinker is ac· 
tually thinking or feeling. Note 
that It's a dangerous temptaUon 
to trick your friends and allies: 
in a camprugn where the PCs' 
party Is rounded on trust and al· 
legla nce. 
such 
a 
Sneaky 
Thinker will probably find him· 
selr cast out or killed for betray· 
ing his ersiwhUe rriends. 
Changing rerson .. llty Types 
The above ad· 
vice Is for players who don't yet 
have a fi rm handle on roleplaying different personalities 
from their own, And the person· 
alitles presented here aren' t 
firm, formal rules which any

character has La follow: they're 
guidelines with no real restrlc· 
tlons placed upon them. 
Naturally. then. It's very ap· 
propriate for you to write up 
more Personality Types appro· 
priate to your campaign . .. Ir. 
indeed. you need to formalize 
them to thai extent. 
It·s also very appropriate for a 
character to change his Personality Type in the course of a 
campaign. Here are some ways 
It can happen: 
The Brash Youth wises up 
and loses his naivete and Inex· 
perlence. This has to happen 
eventually (unless he gelS killed 
before he ever gets wise). His ex· 
periences in the campaign so far 
will probably have a strong Influence on the next type of per· 
sonallty he chooses. Here's a 
note for the other personalities: 
NOlhing short of amnesia ever 
changes a character 
( 0 the 
Brash Youth personality. 
The Crude Crusher isn't 
likely to Change. but a couple of 
Lhings could bring about such a 
change. He could fall In love 
with a more refined partner. 
and . feeling gross and coarse in 
comparison. train himself to be· 
come more sophisticated. (The 
Merry Showoff and Fated Phi· 
losopher become good options 
at this polnt.j He could have 
heavy responsiblJity laJd upon 
him (such as a mHitary officer's 
commission) and be changed by 
It (at which point Lhe Natural 
Leader is a good choice). 
The Dangerous Antagonist 
is only likely to change when 
whatever made him a Dangerous Antagonist originally is resolved. For instance, if he ever 
avenges himself on whomever 
hun him in the first place, he 
could change to a Fated Philosopher or Natural Leader. 
The Doomed Champion Isn't 
likely ever to change. He's 
doomed, after all. But if, in the 
course of the campaign. the OM 
allows him to un-doom or uncurse himself. he could suffer a 
change of personality, He could 
become a Fated Philosopher or 
NaturaJ Leader. He might enjoy 
life so much that he becomes a 
Merry Showoff or Sneaky 
Thinker. He's not likely to become a Dangerous Antagonist 
unless the events which freed 
him from h.ls curse were so 
nasty that they still scarred his 
personality forever. 
The Fated Philosopher isn't 
likely to change. This personality type comes at the eIld of a 
process of personality development. not In the middle. 
The Merry Showof( could saber up under the weight of responsibility. like the Crude 
Crusher. and become a Natural 
Leader. Or he could naturally 
evolve into a Sneaky Thinker 
when it became less and less fulfilling merely to cnterlaJn pea· 
pie and morc fun to manipulate 
them. 
The Natural Leader could 
easily change if dramatic evems 
made him sick and disgusted 
with always being so responsl· 
ble and depcndable. At t.hat 
point. any personality except 
Doomed Champion Is appropri· 
ate, However. unless hc's be· 
come tOlaily amoral, even in his 
new personality he'll probably 
stili have leadership impulses
